Annual Exam - 2024

Code: 65/2/2 (set-2)

MATHEMATICS

Time allowed:3 hours
Maximum Marks:80

General Instructions :

Read the following instructions very carefully and strictly follow them:

  1. This question paper contains 38 questions. All questions are compulsory.
  2. This question paper is divided into five Sections - A, B, C, D and E.
  3. In Section A, Questions no. 1 to 18 are multiple choice questions (MCQs) and questions number 19 and 20 are Assertion-Reason based questions of 1 mark each.
  4. In Section B, Questions no. 21 to 25 are very short answer (VSA) type questions, carrying 2 marks each.
  5. In Section C, Questions no. 26 to 31 are short answer (SA) type questions, carrying 3 marks each.
  6. In Section D, Questions no. 32 to 35 are long answer (LA) type questions carrying 5 marks each.
  7. In Section E, Questions no. 36 to 38 are case study based questions carrying 4 marks each.
  8. There is no overall choice. However, an internal choice has been provided in 2 questions in Section B, 3 questions in Section C, 2 questions in Section D and 2 questions in Section E.
  9. Use of calculators is not allowed.

SECTION A

This section comprises multiple choice questions (MCQs) of 1 mark each.
1.

The number of solutions of differential equation {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}-y=1{/tex}, given that y(0) = 1, is:

a. 0
b. 1
c. 2
d. infinitely many
2.

For any two vectors {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}{/tex} which of the following statements is always true?

a. {tex} \overrightarrow{{a}} \cdot \overrightarrow{{~b}} \geq|\overrightarrow{{a}}||\overrightarrow{{b}}|{/tex}
b. {tex} \overrightarrow{{a}} \cdot \overrightarrow{{~b}}=|\overrightarrow{{a}}||\overrightarrow{{b}}|{/tex}
c. {tex} \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b} \leq|\vec{a}||\vec{b}|{/tex}
d. {tex} \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}<|\vec{a}||\vec{b}|{/tex}
3.

The coordinates of the foot of the perpendicular drawn from the point (0, 1, 2) on the x-axis are given by:

a. (1, 0, 0)
b. (2, 0, 0)
c. {tex} (\sqrt{5}, 0,0){/tex}
d. (0, 0, 0)
4.

The common region determined by all the constraints of a linear programming problem is called:

a. an unbounded region
b. an optimal region
c. a bounded region
d. a feasible region
5.

Let E be an event of a sample space S of an experiment, then P(S|E) =

a. {tex} {P}({~S} \cap {E}){/tex}
b. P(E)
c. 1
d. 0
6.

The number of all scalar matrices of order 3, with each entry -1, 0 or 1, is:

a. 1
b. 3
c. 2
d. 39
7.

{tex}\frac{d}{d x}\left[\cos \left(\log x+e^x\right)\right] \text { at } x=1{/tex} is:

a. -sin e
b. sin e
c. -(1 + e) sin e
d. (1 + e) sin e
8.

The degree of the differential equation {tex}\left(y^{\prime \prime}\right)^2+\left(y^{\prime}\right)^3=x \sin \left(y^{\prime}\right){/tex} is:

a. 1
b. 2
c. 3
d. not defined
9.

The unit vector perpendicular to both vectors {tex}\hat{i}+\hat{k} \text { and } \hat{i}-\hat{k}{/tex} is:

a. {tex} 2 \hat{j}{/tex}
b. {tex} \hat{j}{/tex}
c. {tex} \frac{\hat{i}-\hat{k}}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
d. {tex} \frac{\hat{i}+\hat{k}}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
10.

Direction ratios of a vector parallel to line {tex}\frac{x-1}{2}=-y=\frac{2 z+1}{6}{/tex} are:

a. 2, -1, 6
b. 2, 1, 6
c. 2, 1, 3
d. 2, -1, 3
11.

If F(x) = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} \cos x & -\sin x & 0 \\ \sin x & \cos x & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} and [F(x)]2 = F(kx), then the value of k is:

a. 1
b. 2
c. 0
d. -2
12.

If a line makes an angle of 30o with the positive direction of x-axis, 120o with the positive direction of y-axis, then the angle which it makes with the positive direction of z-axis is:

a. 90o
b. 120o
c. 60o
d. 0o
13.

If the sum of all the elements of a 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 scalar matrix is 9, then the product of all its elements is:

a. 0
b. 9
c. 27
d. 729
14.

Which of the following statements is not true about equivalence classes Ai (i = 1, 2, ...n) formed by an equivalence relation R defined on a set A?

a. {tex} \bigcup_\limits{i=1}^n A_i=A{/tex}
b. {tex} {A}_{{i}} \cap {~A}_{{j}} \neq \phi, {i} \neq {j}{/tex}
c. {tex} x \in A_i \text { and } x \in A_j \Rightarrow A_i=A_j{/tex}
d. All elements of Ai are related to each other, for all i
15.

If {tex}\left|\begin{array}{rrr} -a & b & c \\ \mathrm{a} & -\mathrm{b} & \mathrm{c} \\ \mathrm{a} & \mathrm{~b} & -\mathrm{c} \end{array}\right|={/tex} kabc, then the value of k is:

a. 0
b. 1
c. 2
d. 4
16.

The number of points of discontinuity of f(x) = {tex}\left\{\begin{aligned} |x|+3, & \text { if } x \leq-3 \\ -2 x, & \text { if }-3<x<3 \\ 6 x+2, & \text { if } x \geq 3 \end{aligned}\right.{/tex} is:

a. 0
b. 1
c. 2
d. infinite
17.

The function f(x) = {tex}x^3-3 x^2+12 x-18{/tex} is:

a. strictly decreasing on R
b. strictly increasing on R
c. neither strictly increasing nor strictly decreasing on R
d. strictly decreasing on {tex}(-\infty, 0){/tex}
18.

If {tex}\int_\limits0^2 2 \mathrm{e}^{2 \mathrm{x}} \mathrm{dx}=\int_\limits0^{\mathrm{a}} \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{dx},{/tex} ex dx, the value of ‘a’ is:

a. 1
b. 2
c. 4
d. {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}

Questions number 19 and 20 are Assertion and Reason based questions. Two statements are given, one labelled Assertion (A) and the other labelled Reason (R). Select the correct answer from the codes (A), (B), (C) and (D) as given below.

  1. Both Assertion (A) and Reason (R) are true and Reason (R) is the correct explanation of the Assertion (A).
  2. Both Assertion (A) and Reason (R) are true, but Reason (R) is not
    the correct explanation of the Assertion (A).
  3. Assertion (A) is true, but Reason (R) is false.
  4. Assertion (A) is false, but Reason (R) is true.
19.

Assertion (A): For two non-zero vectors {tex}\vec{a} \text { and } \vec{b}, \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}=\vec{b} \cdot \vec{a} \text {. }{/tex}
Reason (R): For two non-zero vectors {tex}\vec{a} \text { and } \vec{b}, \vec{a} \times \vec{b}=\vec{b} \times \vec{a}{/tex}

a. Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
b. Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
c. A is true but R is false.
d. A is false but R is true.
20.

Assertion (A): For any symmetric matrix A, B' AB is a skew-symmetric matrix.
Reason (R): A square matrix P is skew-symmetric if P' = -P.

a. Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
b. Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
c. A is true but R is false.
d. A is false but R is true.

SECTION B

This section comprises very short answer (VSA) type questions of 2 marks each.
21.

If M and m denote the local maximum and local minimum values of the function f(x) = {tex}x+\frac{1}{x}(x \neq 0) {/tex} respectively, find the value of (M - m).
22.

Evaluate: {tex}\int_\limits0^{a^3} \frac{x^2}{x^6+a^6} d x{/tex}
23.

Show that f(x) = ex - e-x + x - tan-1 x is strictly increasing in its domain.
24.(i)

Find the value of {tex}{\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( { - \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}} \right) + {\cot ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}} \right) + {\tan ^{ - 1}}\left[ {\sin \left( {\frac{{ - \pi }}{2}} \right)} \right]{/tex}.

OR
(ii)

Find the domain of the function f(x) = {tex}\sin ^{-1}\left(x^2-4\right) {/tex}. Also, find its range.
25.(i)

Check the differentiability of f(x) = {tex}|\cos x| \text { at } x=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}.

OR
(ii)

y = A sin 2x + B cos 2x and {tex}\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}-k y=0{/tex}, find the value of k.

SECTION C

This section comprises short answer (SA) type questions of 3 marks each.
26.(i)

Find the particular solution of the differential equation given by {tex}2 x y+y^2-2 x^2 \frac{d y}{d x}=0 ; y=2, \text { when } x=1{/tex}.

OR
(ii)

Find the general solution of the differential equation: {tex}y d x=\left(x+2 y^2\right) d y{/tex}
27.

If vectors {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}, \overrightarrow{\mathrm{~b}}{/tex} and {tex}2 \vec{a}+3 \vec{b}{/tex} are unit vectors, then find the angle between {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}{/tex}.
28.

A pair of dice is thrown simultaneously. If X denotes the absolute difference of numbers obtained on the pair of dice, then find the probability distribution of X.
29.

Find: {tex}\int x^2 \cdot \sin ^{-1}\left(x^{3 / 2}\right) d x{/tex}
30.(i)

If {tex}x^{30} y^{20}=(x+y)^{50} \text {, prove that } \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y}{x} .{/tex}

OR
(ii)

Find {tex}\frac{d y}{d x} \text {, if } 5^x+5^y=5^{x+y}{/tex}.
31.(i)

Evaluate: {tex}\int_\limits{-2}^2 \sqrt{\frac{2-x}{2+x}} d x{/tex}

OR
(ii)

Find: {tex}\int \frac{1}{x\left[(\log x)^2-3 \log x-4\right]} d x{/tex}

SECTION D

This section comprises long answer type questions (LA) of 5 marks each.
32.

Find the value of p for which the lines
{tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}=\lambda \hat{\mathrm{i}}+(2 \lambda+1) \hat{\mathrm{j}}+(3 \lambda+2) \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}=\hat{\mathrm{i}}-3 \mu \hat{\mathrm{j}}+(\mathrm{p} \mu+7) \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}
are perpendicular to each other and also intersect. Also, find the point of intersection of the given lines.
(i)

If {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{lll} 5 & 0 & 4 \\ 2 & 3 & 2 \\ 1 & 2 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} and B-1 = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 3 & 3 \\ 1 & 4 & 3 \\ 1 & 3 & 4 \end{array}\right]{/tex}, find (AB)-1 Also, find | (AB)-1 |.

OR
33.(ii)

Given A = {tex}=\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 2 & 3 & 2 \\ 1 & 1 & 2 \end{array}\right]{/tex}, find A-1. Use it to solve the following system of equations:
{tex} x+y+z=1 {/tex}
{tex} 2 x+3 y+2 z=2 {/tex}
{tex} x+y+2 z=4 {/tex}
34.

If A1 denotes the area of region bounded by y2 = 4x, x = 1 and x-axis in the first quadrant and A2 denotes the area of region bounded by y2 = 4x, x = 4, find A1 : A2.
35.(i)

Show that a function f : R {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} R defined by f(x) = {tex}\frac{2 x}{1+x^2}{/tex} is neither one-one nor onto. Further, find set A so that the given function f : R {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} A becomes an onto function.

OR
(ii)

A relation R is defined on N {tex}\times{/tex} N (where N is the set of natural numbers) as: {tex}(a, b) R(c, d) \Leftrightarrow a-c=b-d{/tex} Show that R is an equivalence relation.

SECTION E

This section comprises 3 case study based questions of 4 marks each.

Case Study - 1
36.

The month of September is celebrated as the Rashtriya Poshan Maah across the country. Following a healthy and well-balanced diet is crucial in order to supply the body with the proper nutrients it needs. A balanced diet also keeps us mentally fit and promotes improved level of energy.

Figure - 1

Figure - 2

A dietician wishes to minimize the cost of a diet involving two types of foods, food {tex}{X}({x} {kg}){/tex} and food {tex}{Y}({y} {kg}){/tex} which are available at the rate of ₹ {tex}16 / {kg}{/tex} and ₹ {tex}20 / {kg}{/tex} respectively. The feasible region satisfying the constraints is shown in Figure-2.

On the basis of the above information, answer the following questions:

  1. Identify and write all the constraints which determine the given feasible region in Figure-2. (2)
  2. If the objective is to minimize cost Z = 16x + 20y, find the values of x and y at which cost is minimum. Also, find minimum cost assuming that minimum cost is possible for the given unbounded region. (2)

Case Study - 2
37.

Airplanes are by far the safest mode of transportation when the number of transported passengers are measured against personal injuries and fatality totals.

Previous records state that the probability of an airplane crash is {tex}0.00001 \%{/tex}. Further, there are {tex}95 \%{/tex} chances that there will be survivors after a plane crash. Assume that in case of no crash, all travellers survive.
Let {tex}{E}_1{/tex} be the event that there is a plane crash and {tex}{E}_2{/tex} be the event that there is no crash. Let A be the event that passengers survive after the journey.
On the basis of the above information, answer the following questions:

  1. Find the probability that the airplane will not crash. (1)
  2. {tex} \text { Find } {P}\left({~A} \mid {E}_1\right)+{P}\left({~A} \mid {E}_2\right) \text {. }{/tex} (1)
    1. Find P(A). (2)
      OR
    2. Find {tex}P\left(E_2 \mid A\right) \text {. }{/tex} (2)

Case Study - 3
38.

Overspeeding increases fuel consumption and decreases fuel economy as a result of tyre rolling friction and air resistance. While vehicles reach optimal fuel economy at different speeds, fuel mileage usually decreases rapidly at speeds above 80 km/h.

The relation between fuel consumption F (l/100 km) and speed V (km/h) under some constraints is given as F = {tex} \frac{{V}^2}{500}-\frac{{V}}{4}+14{/tex}.
On the basis of the above information, answer the following questions:

  1. Find F, when V = 40 km/h. (1)
  2. Find {tex} \frac{{dF}}{{dV}}{/tex}. (1)
  3.  
    1. Find the speed V for which fuel consumption F is minimum. (2)
      OR
    2. Find the quantity of fuel required to travel 600 km at the speed V at which {tex} \frac{{dF}}{{dV}}{/tex} = -0.01. (2)

MATHEMATICS

Time allowed:3 hours
Maximum Marks:80

General Instructions :

Read the following instructions very carefully and strictly follow them:

  1. This question paper contains 38 questions. All questions are compulsory.
  2. This question paper is divided into five Sections - A, B, C, D and E.
  3. In Section A, Questions no. 1 to 18 are multiple choice questions (MCQs) and questions number 19 and 20 are Assertion-Reason based questions of 1 mark each.
  4. In Section B, Questions no. 21 to 25 are very short answer (VSA) type questions, carrying 2 marks each.
  5. In Section C, Questions no. 26 to 31 are short answer (SA) type questions, carrying 3 marks each.
  6. In Section D, Questions no. 32 to 35 are long answer (LA) type questions carrying 5 marks each.
  7. In Section E, Questions no. 36 to 38 are case study based questions carrying 4 marks each.
  8. There is no overall choice. However, an internal choice has been provided in 2 questions in Section B, 3 questions in Section C, 2 questions in Section D and 2 questions in Section E.
  9. Use of calculators is not allowed.

SECTION A

This section comprises multiple choice questions (MCQs) of 1 mark each.
1.

The number of solutions of differential equation {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}-y=1{/tex}, given that y(0) = 1, is:

a. 0
b. 1
c. 2
d. infinitely many
Correct Answer: (b) 1
Explanation:

The differential equation {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}-y=1{/tex} is a first-order linear equation. Its general solution is {tex}y(x)=C e^x+e^x-1{/tex}. Given the initial condition {tex}y(0)=1{/tex}, we find {tex}C=0{/tex}. Thus, there is exactly one solution: {tex}y(x)=e^x-1{/tex}. The answer is 1 .

 1
2.

For any two vectors {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}{/tex} which of the following statements is always true?

a. {tex} \overrightarrow{{a}} \cdot \overrightarrow{{~b}} \geq|\overrightarrow{{a}}||\overrightarrow{{b}}|{/tex}
b. {tex} \overrightarrow{{a}} \cdot \overrightarrow{{~b}}=|\overrightarrow{{a}}||\overrightarrow{{b}}|{/tex}
c. {tex} \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b} \leq|\vec{a}||\vec{b}|{/tex}
d. {tex} \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}<|\vec{a}||\vec{b}|{/tex}
Correct Answer: (c) {tex} \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b} \leq|\vec{a}||\vec{b}|{/tex}
Explanation:

The correct answer is {tex}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b} \leq|\vec{a}||\vec{b}|{/tex}. This follows from the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality, which states that the dot product of two vectors is less than or equal to the product of their magnitudes. Equality holds only when the vectors are parallel.

 1
3.

The coordinates of the foot of the perpendicular drawn from the point (0, 1, 2) on the x-axis are given by:

a. (1, 0, 0)
b. (2, 0, 0)
c. {tex} (\sqrt{5}, 0,0){/tex}
d. (0, 0, 0)
Correct Answer: (d) (0, 0, 0)
Explanation:

The foot of the perpendicular from the point (0, 1, 2) to the x-axis has the same x-coordinate (0) but zero y and z-coordinates. Thus, the coordinates are (0, 0, 0)

4.

The common region determined by all the constraints of a linear programming problem is called:

a. an unbounded region
b. an optimal region
c. a bounded region
d. a feasible region
Correct Answer: (d) a feasible region
Explanation:

a feasible region

 1
5.

Let E be an event of a sample space S of an experiment, then P(S|E) =

a. {tex} {P}({~S} \cap {E}){/tex}
b. P(E)
c. 1
d. 0
Correct Answer: (c) 1
Explanation:

The conditional probability {tex}P(S \mid E){/tex} represents the probability of the sample space {tex}S{/tex} given event {tex}E{/tex}. Since {tex}S{/tex} is the entire sample space, it always occurs, leading to:
{tex} P(S \mid E)=1{/tex}
Thus, the correct answer is 1.

 1
6.

The number of all scalar matrices of order 3, with each entry -1, 0 or 1, is:

a. 1
b. 3
c. 2
d. 39
Correct Answer: (b) 3
Explanation:

A scalar matrix of order 3 has the form cI, where I is the identity matrix and c is a constant Given c can be -1, 0, or 1 , there are 3 distinct scalar matrices: -I, 0 and I. Therefore, the answer is 3.

 1
7.

{tex}\frac{d}{d x}\left[\cos \left(\log x+e^x\right)\right] \text { at } x=1{/tex} is:

a. -sin e
b. sin e
c. -(1 + e) sin e
d. (1 + e) sin e
Correct Answer: (c) -(1 + e) sin e
Explanation:

To find {tex}\frac{d}{d x}\left[\cos \left(\log x+e^x\right)\right]{/tex} at {tex}x=1{/tex}, use the chain rule:
{tex} \frac{d}{d x}=-\sin \left(\log x+e^x\right) \cdot\left(\frac{1}{x}+e^x\right) .{/tex}
At {tex}x=1, \log 1=0{/tex} and {tex}e^1=e{/tex}, so the expression simplifies to {tex}-\sin e \cdot(1+e){/tex}. Thus, the answer is {tex}-(1+e) \sin e{/tex}.

8.

The degree of the differential equation {tex}\left(y^{\prime \prime}\right)^2+\left(y^{\prime}\right)^3=x \sin \left(y^{\prime}\right){/tex} is:

a. 1
b. 2
c. 3
d. not defined
Correct Answer: (d) not defined
Explanation:

{tex}\left(y^{\prime \prime}\right)^2+\left(y^{\prime}\right)^3=x \sin \left(\cdot y^{\prime}\right){/tex} is.
{tex}\because{/tex} it is not diff eq.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} degree of diff is undefined / Not defined

 1
9.

The unit vector perpendicular to both vectors {tex}\hat{i}+\hat{k} \text { and } \hat{i}-\hat{k}{/tex} is:

a. {tex} 2 \hat{j}{/tex}
b. {tex} \hat{j}{/tex}
c. {tex} \frac{\hat{i}-\hat{k}}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
d. {tex} \frac{\hat{i}+\hat{k}}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
Correct Answer: (b) {tex} \hat{j}{/tex}
Explanation:

To find a unit vector perpendicular to both {tex}\hat{i}+\hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\hat{i}-\hat{k}{/tex}, we use the cross product:
{tex} (\hat{i}+\hat{k}) \times(\hat{i}-\hat{k})=\hat{j}(-1-1)=-2 \hat{j}{/tex}
The unit vector is
{tex} \frac{-2 \hat{j}}{2}=-\hat{j}{/tex}
Thus, the correct answer is {tex}\hat{j}{/tex}.

 1
10.

Direction ratios of a vector parallel to line {tex}\frac{x-1}{2}=-y=\frac{2 z+1}{6}{/tex} are:

a. 2, -1, 6
b. 2, 1, 6
c. 2, 1, 3
d. 2, -1, 3
Correct Answer: (d) 2, -1, 3
Explanation:

The given equation of the line is:
{tex} \frac{x-1}{2}=-y=\frac{2 z+1}{6}{/tex}
This represents the parametric form of the line. We can break this down into parametric equations by introducing a parameter {tex}t{/tex} :
{tex} \frac{x-1}{2}=t \Rightarrow x=2 t+1 {/tex}
{tex} -y=t \Rightarrow y=-t {/tex}
{tex} \frac{2 z+1}{6}=t \Rightarrow 2 z+1=6 t \Rightarrow z=3 t-\frac{1}{2} {/tex}
Thus, the parametric equations are:
{tex} x=2 t+1, y=-t, z=3 t-\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
The direction ratios of the vector parallel to the line are the coefficients of {tex}t{/tex} in the parametric equations. Therefore, the direction ratios are:
{tex} \text { Direction ratios }=(2,-1,3){/tex}
So, the correct answer is:
2, -1, 3.

 1
11.

If F(x) = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} \cos x & -\sin x & 0 \\ \sin x & \cos x & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} and [F(x)]2 = F(kx), then the value of k is:

a. 1
b. 2
c. 0
d. -2
Correct Answer: (b) 2
Explanation:

To find {tex}k{/tex} such that {tex}[F(x)]^2=F(k x){/tex}, we compute {tex}[F(x)]^2{/tex} :{tex} [F(x)]^2=F(x) F(x)=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} \cos ^2 x-\sin ^2 x & -2 \sin x \cos x & 0 \\ 2 \sin x \cos x & \cos ^2 x-\sin ^2 x & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]=F(2 x){/tex}
Thus, {tex}k=2{/tex}. The correct answer is 2.

 1
12.

If a line makes an angle of 30o with the positive direction of x-axis, 120o with the positive direction of y-axis, then the angle which it makes with the positive direction of z-axis is:

a. 90o
b. 120o
c. 60o
d. 0o
Correct Answer: (a) 90o
Explanation:

To find the angle with the z-axis, we use the relation:
{tex} \cos ^2 \alpha+\cos ^2 \beta+\cos ^2 \gamma=1{/tex}
where {tex}\alpha=30^{\circ}{/tex} and {tex}\beta=120^{\circ}{/tex}. Calculating, we get:
{tex} \cos ^2 30^{\circ}=\frac{3}{4}, \cos ^2 120^{\circ}=\frac{1}{4}{/tex}
Thus,
{tex} \frac{3}{4}+\frac{1}{4}+\cos ^2 \gamma=1 \Longrightarrow \cos ^2 \gamma=0 \Longrightarrow \gamma=90^{\circ} .{/tex}
So, the angle with the z-axis is 90o.

 1
13.

If the sum of all the elements of a 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 scalar matrix is 9, then the product of all its elements is:

a. 0
b. 9

17.

The function f(x) = {tex}x^3-3 x^2+12 x-18{/tex} is:

a. strictly decreasing on R
b. strictly increasing on R
c. neither strictly increasing nor strictly decreasing on R
d. strictly decreasing on {tex}(-\infty, 0){/tex}
Correct Answer: (b) strictly increasing on R
Explanation:

To determine the behavior of the function {tex}f(x)=x^3-3 x^2+12 x-18{/tex}, we first compute its derivative:
{tex} f^{\prime}(x)=3 x^2-6 x+12 \text {. }{/tex}
Next, we analyze the derivative to find critical points and its sign:

  1. Finding the roots of {tex}f^{\prime}(x){/tex}:
    {tex} f^{\prime}(x)=3\left(x^2-2 x+4\right){/tex}
    The discriminant of the quadratic {tex}x^2-2 x+4{/tex} is:
    {tex} D=(-2)^2-4 \cdot 1 \cdot 4=4-16=-12{/tex}
    Since the discriminant is negative, {tex}f^{\prime}(x){/tex} has no real roots, which means it does not change sign.
  2. Analyzing the sign of {tex}f^{\prime}(x){/tex}: The quadratic x2 - 2x + 4 opens upwards (as the coefficient of x2 is positive) and is always positive because it has no real roots. Therefore, {tex}f^{\prime}(x){/tex} > 0 for all x.

Since {tex}f^{\prime}(x)>0{/tex} for all {tex}x \in R{/tex}, the function {tex}f(x){/tex} is strictly increasing on the entire real line.
Thus, the correct answer is strictly increasing on R

{tex} \int_\limits0^2 2 e^{2 x} d x=\left[e^{2 x}\right]_0^2=e^4-1{/tex}

Find the particular solution of the differential equation given by {tex}2 x y+y^2-2 x^2 \frac{d y}{d x}=0 ; y=2, \text { when } x=1{/tex}.

Solution:

Given differential equation can be written as
{tex} \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{2 x y+y^2}{2 x^2}=\frac{y}{x}+\frac{y^2}{2 x^2}{/tex}
Let {tex}y=v x \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex}
The equation becomes
{tex} x \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{1}{2} v^2 {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{d v}{v^2}=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{d x}{x} {/tex}
Integrating both sides, we get
{tex} \frac{-1}{v}=\frac{1}{2} \log |x|+C {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow-\frac{x}{y}=\frac{1}{2} \log |x|+C {/tex}
{tex} y=2, x=1 \text { gives } C=-\frac{1}{2} {/tex}
The particular solution is
{tex} -\frac{x}{y}=\frac{1}{2} \log |x|-\frac{1}{2} \text { or, } y=\frac{2 x}{1-\log |x|}{/tex}

If {tex}x^{30} y^{20}=(x+y)^{50} \text {, prove that } \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y}{x} .{/tex}

Solution:

Taking log of both sides, we get
{tex} 30 \log x+20 \log y=50 \log (x+y){/tex}
Differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get

{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y}{x} {/tex}

35.(i)

Show that a function f : R {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} R defined by f(x) = {tex}\frac{2 x}{1+x^2}{/tex} is neither one-one nor onto. Further, find set A so that the given function f : R {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} A becomes an onto function.

Solution:

Let {tex}f\left(x_1\right)=f\left(x_2\right){/tex} for some {tex}x_1, x_2 \in R{/tex}
Then {tex}\frac{2 x_1}{1+x_1^2}=\frac{2 x_2}{1+x_2^2}{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow x_1+x_1 x_2^2=x_2+x_1^2 x_2 {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow\left(x_1-x_2\right)-x_1 x_2\left(x_1-x_2\right)=0 {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow\left(x_1-x_2\right)\left(1-x_1 x_2\right)=0 {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow x_1-x_2=0 \text { or } 1-x_1 x_2=0 {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow x_1=x_2 \text { or } x_1 x_2=1, \text { so if } x_1 x_2=1, x_1 \neq x_2 {/tex}
Hence f is not one -one
Let {tex}{y}={f}({x}){/tex} where {tex}x \in R{/tex}
Then {tex}y=\frac{2 x}{1+x^2}{/tex}. Here, for {tex}{x}=0, {y}=0{/tex}
{tex} \text { If } y \neq 0 \text {, then } y=\frac{2 x}{1+x^2} {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow y x^2-2 x+y=0 {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow x=\frac{2 \pm \sqrt{4-4 y^2}}{2 y} {/tex}
For x to be real, {tex}4-4 y^2 \geq 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow y^2 \leq 1 {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow-1 \leq y \leq 1 {/tex}
Hence, range {tex}=[-1,1] \neq{/tex} codomain
Hence, f is not onto.
For the given function to become onto, {tex}A=[-1,1]{/tex}

A relation R is defined on N {tex}\times{/tex} N (where N is the set of natural numbers) as: {tex}(a, b) R(c, d) \Leftrightarrow a-c=b-d{/tex} Show that R is an equivalence relation.

Solution:

Let {tex}(a, b) \in N \times N{/tex}
We have
{tex} a-a=b-b{/tex}
This implies that {tex}({a}, {b}) {R}({a}, {b}) \forall(a, b) \in N \times N{/tex}
Hence R is reflexive
Let {tex}({a}, {b}) {R}({c}, {d}){/tex} for some {tex}(a, b),(c, d) \in N \times N{/tex}
Then {tex}a-c=b-d{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow c-a=d-b {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow({c}, {~d}) {R}({a}, {~b}) {/tex}
Hence, R is symmetric.
Let {tex}({a}, {b}) {R}({c}, {d}),({c}, {d}) {R}({e}, {f}){/tex} for some {tex}(a, b),(c, d),(e, f) \in N \times N{/tex}
Then {tex}a-c=b-d, c-e=d-f{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow a-c+c-e=b-d+d-f {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow a-e=b-f {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow({a}, {~b}) {R}({e}, {f}) {/tex}
Hence, R is transitive
Thus, R is an equivalence relation.
